name: Giraffe
summary: Giraffes are the tallest land animals on Earth. They use their long necks and tongues to browse leaves high in trees and live in loose herds across African...
metaTitle: Giraffe Facts | Habitat, Height, Diet & Photos
metaDescription: Discover giraffe facts with photos, long-neck adaptations, habitat, diet, calves, and conservation. Learn how tall giraffes are and why they need savanna trees.
searchIntents: why do giraffes have long necks

lifespan.captivity: Up to 30 years in human care

funFacts: A giraffe's tongue can be about 18 to 20 inches long.
Giraffes are the tallest land animals in the world.
Each giraffe has a unique patch pattern.
Newborn calves can stand not long after birth.
Giraffes sleep only for short periods at a time.
Male giraffes sometimes swing their necks in contests called necking.

faq.question: What is a giraffe?
faq.answer: A giraffe is a tall African mammal with a very long neck and legs. It is the tallest land animal on Earth.

faq.question: Where do giraffes live?
faq.answer: Giraffes live in African savannas, open woodlands, and grasslands where trees grow far apart.

faq.question: What do giraffes eat?
faq.answer: Giraffes are herbivores. They mainly eat leaves, shoots, flowers, and seed pods from tall trees such as acacias.

faq.question: How tall is a giraffe?
faq.answer: Adult giraffes can grow to about 14 to 19 feet tall. Males are usually taller than females.

faq.question: Why do giraffes have long tongues?
faq.answer: A giraffe's long tongue helps it pull leaves from thorny branches high above the ground.

faq.question: What is a baby giraffe called?
faq.answer: A baby giraffe is called a calf. Calves can stand and walk soon after they are born.

faq.question: Do giraffes live alone?
faq.answer: Giraffes usually live in loose groups called herds. The group may change as animals move around for food.

faq.question: Are giraffes endangered?
faq.answer: Giraffes are listed as Vulnerable overall. Habitat loss and broken-up migration routes have reduced some wild populations.
